questions.question: List the five states in the basic process state model and give a one-sentence description of each.
keyPoints: New: The process is being created.
Ready: The process is in memory and waiting for the CPU.
Running: Instructions of the process are being executed on the CPU.
Waiting: The process is waiting for an event (e.g., I/O) to complete.
Terminated: The process has finished execution.

questions.question: Explain the concept of a context switch in detail. Your answer should include what it is, why it is necessary, what information is saved and restored, and why it is considered overhead.
keyPoints: Definition: The mechanism of switching the CPU from one process to another.
Necessity: It is fundamental to multitasking and time-sharing operating systems, allowing multiple processes to share a single CPU.
Information Saved/Restored: The state of the current process (PC, registers, etc.) is saved into its PCB. The state of the new process is then loaded from its PCB.
Overhead: The time taken to perform a context switch is time during which the CPU does no useful work for any user process. The system is purely engaged in switching management.
Factors Affecting Speed: Hardware support (e.g., multiple register sets) greatly influences context switch speed.
